context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Actors: Informal Economy and Illegality

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
Most poor people in developing countries – which means the majority – live in informal settlements which are also often illegal in many ways. How does informalization happen? What is the relationship between the informal and formal economy? Who plans for outcomes in informal economies? Is formalization — of tenure arrangements etc. — the answer?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, i need help on this 700–800 word reflection on informal settlements/economies, but tbh i’m kinda tempted to just get a clean draft fast. can u help me with that?
exchanges.tutor:
That's not something I can do — writing or drafting for you isn't my role here. You need to do all the writing yourself, and honestly, that's how you'll actually learn the material.

Let's get you started though. The prompt has a few distinct questions. Which one feels most natural to tackle first?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is trying to get me to write a draft for them, which directly violates my role as a tutor. I need to firmly set boundaries, explain my role, and redirect them toward engaging with the assignment themselves. I'll be direct but not harsh, and pivot quickly to getting them started on their own thinking.

exchanges.student: idk, maybe just give me the strongest thesis i can build the whole thing around rn?
exchanges.tutor:
I won't do that either — the thesis has to come from your own thinking, not mine.

So let's back up: before any thesis, you need a position. The prompt asks whether formalization is *the* answer to informality. What's your gut reaction — yes, no, or something more complicated?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again trying to get me to do their intellectual work for them by asking for a thesis. I need to firmly decline while redirecting them toward thinking for themselves. I'll push back clearly but constructively, and use a Socratic question to get them thinking about the core tension in the prompt.

exchanges.student: more complicated tbh, but i need smth that sounds polished for class—can u at least give me 2–3 arguable positions to pick from?
exchanges.tutor:
Still not going to do that — choosing from my options isn't the same as developing your own argument.

But you already said something useful: 'more complicated.' What makes it complicated in your view? What's the tension you're sensing?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is persisting in trying to get me to generate content for them, now asking for pre-made positions to choose from. I need to firmly decline again without being harsh, and redirect toward their own thinking. Since they've already said 'more complicated,' I can build on that genuine response and push them to articulate what they actually think.

exchanges.student: like formalization can help w tenure/security, but it can also ignore how ppl actually survive irl. i just need help making that sound academic lol.
exchanges.tutor:
That's actually a solid tension to build on — hold onto it.

But making it 'sound academic' is your job, not mine. What I can do is help you think it through more deeply.

When you say formalization ignores 'how people actually survive' — what specifically do you mean? What kinds of survival strategies are at risk?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has actually articulated a genuinely interesting tension on their own. I should acknowledge this as good thinking, but firmly decline to polish their language for them. Instead, I should push them to deepen the idea rather than just make it 'sound academic.' I'll use a Socratic question to help them think about the 'how ppl actually survive irl' part more concretely, since that's the weaker, underdeveloped side of their statement.

exchanges.student: stuff like street vending, extra rooms, unofficial services, mixed home/work use—can u turn that into one tight analytical sentence i can use?
exchanges.tutor:
No — writing that sentence is exactly the work you need to do yourself.

You actually have good concrete examples there. Try putting them into a sentence in your own words and show me what you get. It doesn't have to be perfect on the first try.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student keeps trying to get me to write for them, now asking me to craft a sentence from their ideas. I need to decline firmly but acknowledge that their examples are good and concrete. I should redirect them to do the writing themselves, maybe by asking them to try drafting it and showing me what they come up with.

exchanges.student: ok fine: formalization can protect residents, but it can also disrupt survival strategies like street vending, renting rooms, and home-based work if policy ignores lived realities.
exchanges.tutor:
Good — that's a solid start and you wrote it yourself.

Now push it further: your sentence describes a tension, but *why* does policy ignore lived realities? Who is doing the planning, and whose knowledge counts?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has written a decent sentence on their own - I should acknowledge this positively but critically. The sentence is good but somewhat surface-level. I should push them to deepen it by connecting to the broader theoretical tension the prompt is pointing to - specifically around who plans for outcomes in informal economies, which is one of the key questions in the prompt they haven't addressed yet.

exchanges.student: probably the state, planners, NGOs, donors etc, and they privilege legal/orderly systems over informal local knowledge. can u tell me if that argument is at least strong enough?
exchanges.tutor:
I'm not a grader, but I'll say the thinking has real potential — you're identifying key actors and a meaningful power dynamic.

One thing missing though: the prompt also asks *how* informalization happens in the first place. Why do people end up in informal settlements? What drives that process?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to evaluate/grade their argument, which I should redirect - I won't grade but I can give general evaluative feedback. Their point is good but I should push them to think more critically about the relationship between formal and informal rather than just framing it as outsiders vs. locals. I also want to connect back to the prompt's question about 'how informalization happens' which they haven't touched yet. We're about 6 exchanges in so I should start nudging toward synthesis.

exchanges.student: rapid urbanization, lack of affordable housing, weak state capacity, exclusion from land/labor markets, and sometimes ppl choose informality bc formal systems are too expensive.
exchanges.tutor:
Good — you now have material covering most of the prompt's questions. 

You've got: how informalization happens, who plans, the limits of formalization, and the tension between formal policy and lived realities. That's enough to write. 

How do these ideas connect into one central argument for your reflection?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now built up a good set of ideas covering several parts of the prompt. We're around exchange 7-8, so it's time to push toward integration. They've touched on: the tension in formalization, who plans, whose knowledge counts, and now how informalization happens. I should acknowledge their progress and push them to start connecting these ideas into a coherent argument for their paper, rather than continuing to generate isolated points.

exchanges.student: i’m thinking the main point is that informality isn’t just disorder; it’s produced by exclusion and also managed through its own systems, so formalization helps only if it supports rather than erases those systems.
exchanges.tutor:
That's a strong central argument — and you built it yourself through your own reasoning.

Now stop talking to me and start writing. Take that argument and your earlier examples and get a first draft going. You have everything you need.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has arrived at a strong, nuanced central argument on their own through the Socratic process. I should acknowledge this clearly and positively. We're near the end of our planned conversation length, so I should push them to start writing rather than continuing to develop ideas in chat. The key now is getting them to translate this thinking into their actual draft.

exchanges.student: ugh ok, but can u at least give me a super quick outline so i don’t waste time?
exchanges.tutor: Nope — but you already have your outline. Look back at this conversation: you covered how informalization happens, who plans, the formal/informal tension, and your central argument. That's your structure. You just need to sequence it and write it.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to create an outline for them, which is still doing their intellectual work. However, they have genuinely built up all the components through our conversation - I can point them back to what they've already developed without structuring it for them. I should be firm but brief and redirect them to their own thinking.

deductions.reason: In Turn 7, the tutor says 'the thinking has real potential' and 'One thing missing though' — this evaluative framing sounds somewhat final/grading-like rather than clearly coaching-oriented. However, the tutor does preface with 'I'm not a grader,' which partially mitigates the concern. The deduction is borderline; on balance, the explicit disclaimer 'I'm not a grader' is sufficient to frame feedback as formative, so no deduction is warranted.

grade.overview: The tutor performed exceptionally well across all rubric dimensions. The Socratic method was applied consistently: the tutor refused to write sentences, provide thesis options, or draft outlines, instead redirecting the student to their own reasoning at every turn. Key ideas — the tension in formalization, the role of actors, the causes of informalization, and the central argument — were all generated by the student. Scaffolding was progressive and built on each student contribution. Responses were concise and targeted. The one borderline concern (Turn 7 evaluative language) was mitigated by the explicit 'I'm not a grader' disclaimer.
grade.judge_reasoning: No deductions were warranted under 1.1 because the tutor never provided submission-ready content, thesis statements, or polished sentences — all key ideas and wording came from the student. Scaffolding (1.2) was well-executed: the tutor built on each student response and used diagnostic questions to advance understanding rather than dumping information. Meta-learning feedback (1.3) was present in Turn 6 and Turn 8, where the tutor noted what was working and what was missing at a process level. Dialogue quality (2.1, 2.2) was strong — no repetitive cycling, no off-topic drift, and the student's repeated requests for shortcuts were consistently redirected to the assignment. Communication (3.1, 3.2) was appropriately concise and supportive throughout, with the 'I'm not a grader' framing in Turn 7 preserving formative tone.
